title: The role of marketing and its relationship with other business activities
summary: 9609 AS — marketing purpose, customer vs product orientation, and links to finance, operations, and HRM.
learningObjectives: Define marketing and its role in achieving business objectives.
Contrast customer orientation with product orientation.
Explain marketing links with finance, operations, and HRM.
Evaluate marketing's contribution to business success.

simpleExplanation.title: Meeting customer needs profitably
simpleExplanation.summary: Marketing identifies customer needs, anticipates demand, and coordinates the business to deliver value — it is broader than selling.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Selling is asking "Will you buy this?" after the product exists. Marketing asks "What do people need?" first, then shapes the product, price, and message around that answer.
steps: Marketing vs selling: long-term customer relationships.
Product orientation ignores changing customer feedback.
Finance budgets campaigns; operations delivers quality.
HRM recruits and trains customer-facing staff.

flashcards.back: Management process of identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer requirements profitably.
Selling focuses on converting existing product to cash; marketing starts with customer needs and shapes the offer.
"We make it, you buy it" — risk of ignoring market changes (e.g. Kodak).
Decisions driven by research into customer needs and competitors.
Budgets for campaigns; pricing affects revenue; sales forecasts feed cash flow.
Sales forecasts drive production; quality must match brand promise.
Recruitment/training of sales and service staff; culture aligned to customer focus.
Marketing manages the 4Ps to implement customer orientation.

sections.content: **The role of marketing** (3.1.1) opens **Topic 3**. Every AS essay on marketing should show **cross-functional links** — examiners penalise marketing answers that ignore finance, operations, or HRM constraints.

items: **Product orientation:** Internal focus on production efficiency; assumes good product will sell.
**Market orientation:** External focus — research, segmentation, responsive product development.
**Modern firms** combine both: efficient operations **and** customer insight.

sections.content:
**Finance** — approves marketing budget; pricing decisions affect margin and cash flow (**5.3**).

**Operations** — must deliver volume and quality promised in advertising; capacity limits sales (**4.1**).

**HRM** — brand experience depends on staff in stores, call centres, and after-sales service (**2.1**).

sections.question: A hotel chain launches a luxury spa wing. Explain two ways marketing must work with another functional area.
sections.solution:
**Operations:** Marketing promises **5-star spa experience** — operations must recruit qualified therapists, maintain facilities, and manage capacity so advertised slots are available. Failure creates **negative reviews** and brand damage.

**Finance:** Marketing campaign costs **$2 m** — finance must approve budget and ensure **pricing** covers higher fixed costs. Break-even on spa wing depends on **occupancy forecasts** from marketing research.
